blocks.text: In this step, the interviewer wants to see if you can describe the data your toast system works with: how a single toast is represented, how the global toast list is stored, and which UI states (timers, variants, placement, accessibility) you treat as first-class data instead of hidden logic.

**How you should talk about data:**
"I’ll keep a simple model: a `Toast` interface for each notification, a `ToastState` for the global list, and a `ToastConfig` for defaults like placement and duration. Each toast knows its id, variant, message, lifetime, and placement."

**What the interviewer listens for:**
- You define a clear shape for a toast, not just "some object".
- You explicitly model things like type, placement and duration.
- You keep the global list and configuration small and predictable.
- You avoid mixing DOM or timer handles directly into the data model.

blocks.title: How you should act here
blocks.text: Name your main types out loud and keep them UI-focused: "Toast", "ToastState", maybe a "ToastConfig". Show that you’re modeling what the UI actually needs to render and manage toasts, not designing a backend schema.

blocks.title: Core entities you should define
rows: id, variant, message, description?, placement, durationMs, autoDismiss, createdAt, ariaRole, action?
"Each toast is a small object with an `id`, a `variant` like success/error, message text, optional description, placement, duration, auto-dismiss flag, ARIA role, and optional action callback."
"`ToastState` is the global slice: an array of active toasts plus config like default placement, default duration and how many toasts can be visible at once."
placement, durationMs, ariaRole, maxVisible, duplicatePolicy, overflowPolicy

"When calling `toast.success(message, options?)`, I’d accept an options object that partially overrides defaults like placement, duration, ARIA role, and action behavior while provider config controls max visible and overflow policy."

blocks.title: Things you should explicitly include in the model
items: A **unique id** per toast so you can remove it reliably.
A **variant** field for visual style and icon (success/error/etc.).
A resolved **placement** field to control where it appears.
An **ARIA role** (`status`/`alert`) for screen reader behavior.
An optional **action** object for interactive toasts.

blocks.title: Things you should avoid putting into the data model
items: Raw DOM nodes or refs inside the toast object.
Actual timer/timeout handles in the model (keep them alongside the component logic or in a separate map).
Random UI-only flags that can be derived (e.g. computing "isOld" from `createdAt` instead of storing another boolean).
Mixing unrelated global UI state (like modals) into `ToastState`.
Overcomplicating the schema for a simple notification use case.

blocks.title: How toast data typically changes over time

steps.title: 1. Add toast
steps.text: User action triggers `toast.success("Saved")`. Under the hood, a new `Toast` object is created with a unique `id`, `variant = 'success'`, `message`, `createdAt`, and duration/placement from defaults or options. It is appended to `ToastState.toasts`.

steps.title: 2. Start lifetime
steps.text: When the ToastItem mounts, it starts an auto-dismiss timer if `autoDismiss` is true. The visual component reads everything it needs from the `Toast` object.

steps.title: 3. Manual or auto dismiss
steps.text: If the user clicks the close button or the timer fires, the system dispatches a `removeToast(id)` action that filters the `Toast` out of `ToastState.toasts`.

steps.title: 4. Optional action
steps.text: If the toast has an action (e.g. "Undo"), clicking it runs the action callback and then dismisses the toast by removing it from state.

items.label: Must-have entities
items.helperText: If you clearly define these two, the rest of the design becomes much easier to explain.

items.label: Key idea
items.helperText: Variant, placement, auto-dismiss and ARIA role all live on the toast, not as scattered magic logic.

items.label: Good signal
items.helperText: If another engineer can understand your model from a short interface snippet, you’re on the right track.

blocks.title: Key message to land
blocks.text: A solid toast data model treats each toast as a small, self-contained piece of state with clear fields for variant, placement, lifetime, and accessibility. This keeps the rendering logic simple and makes the global store easy to reason about.
